author: garyrong
content:
I also reached out to Maria for clarification on the same issue.

I think there are a few possible approaches on the table:

- Reservoir-based refund: all pre-charged state gas is refunded back to the reservoir. In the case of an exceptional halt, the final reservoir becomes: reservoir_remaining + state_refunds.

- Source-based refund: state gas is refunded back to the source it was originally charged from.

- Reservoir-based refund + frame-level reservoir resetting: all pre-charged state gas is refunded to the reservoir, but upon an exceptional halt, the reservoir is reset to its initial value for that frame.

I guess (3) is probably easier to reason about. For (1), as Maria pointed out, the ultimate gasUsed may vary depending on the ordering between state-gas charging and the exceptional halt. Since gas is moved from the regular gas pool into the reservoir and any amount that has already spilled over is preserved. For (2), in the event of an exceptional halt, the regular-portion of state gas is returned and preserved and all other regular is fully burnt, it's a bit hard to reason about.

author: chfast
content:
Yes, I also like 3 the most: same implementations complexity but we keep properties of calls ending with exceptional halt. 

Option 1 (and all current implementations) has "reservoir inflation effect". I dig up the spec history and this PR "clarifies" it into 1. https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/pull/11328. However, this wording hasn't survived in the current spec and now this is vague again.

author: chfast
content: Moreover, I think we can restore the same property also for REVERT. At the end of a frame we have all information needed to compute the spill amount and we can return it to the regular_gas_left.

author: garyrong
content:
Well, in this case I think we need to go (2)?

For Revert: we calculate the spilled over portion and give it back to the original source (regular)
For Halt: we calculate the spilled over portion  and burn it alongside the regular

author: lu_pinto
content:
IMO It would be nice to have a step by step description in the EIP of what's happening at key points of the execution: top-level frame enter boundary, frame.depth >=1 enter boundary, individual instruction execution, frame return (success, halt, revert). I also agree that it is not very clear to the reader what's actually happening. I know it is a lot more work but this EIP deeply changes things and it's not easy to reason about with a general description.
This is even a win long term, as for people and AI agents reading the spec after the fork to reason what's actually happening in the EVM.

author: garyrong
content:
Re-iterate the two approaches on the table then:

- Reservoir-based refund: all pre-charged state gas is refunded back to the state-gas reservoir.
  - In the case of an exceptional halt
    - reservoir = reservoir_remaining + state_refunds
    - regular = 0
  - In the case of a revert
    - reservoir = reservoir_remaining + state_refunds
    - regular gas is unchanged

- Source-based refund: state gas is refunded to the source from which it was originally charged.
  - In the case of an exceptional halt
    - reservoir = reservoir_remaining + state_refunds - spilled
    - regular = 0 (spilled is also burnt)
  - In the case of a revert:
    - reservoir = reservoir_remaining + state_refunds - spilled
    - regular = regular_remaining + spilled (spilled is preserved)

author: misilva73
content: What I recall from the interop discussions was the following. The issue of giving the state refill to the place where it was taken from was mostly implementation complexity. While giving the refill back to the reservoir always had the issue of allowing transactions to move gas from gas left to the reservoir. At the time I did not have a strong view on either option, but I remember <@429719902363058177> was very in favour of doing the reservoir refill.

author: misilva73
content: The only thing I felt strongly about was that the case I share above where depending on the place where the recert happened within nested calls, the total ga used would be different. That cannot happen. So, we added that exception for reverts where the reservoir is set back to it's original value
